mission.title: Install and Configure MySQL Operator for Kubernetes (Oracle)
mission.description: The MySQL Operator for Kubernetes automates the management of MySQL InnoDB Cluster setups within a Kubernetes environment. It simplifies tasks such as deployment, scaling, and backup, making it ideal for users who need to manage MySQL databases at scale. Key trade-offs include the complexity of setup and the need for proper resource allocation to ensure performance.

steps.title: Add the MySQL Operator Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official MySQL Operator Helm repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add mysql-operator https://mysql.github.io/mysql-operator/

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the MySQL Operator repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the MySQL Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the MySQL Operator in a dedicated namespace.

```bash

helm install mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --create-namespace --version 9.0.0

```

> Replace '9.0.0' with the desired version if necessary.

steps.title: Verify the Operator installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the MySQL Operator is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> You should see the deployment status as '1/1' indicating it's running.

steps.title: Create a MySQL root user secret
steps.description:
This step creates a Kubernetes secret that contains the credentials for the MySQL root user.

```bash

kubectl create secret generic mypwds --from-literal=rootUser=root --from-literal=rootHost=% --from-literal=rootPassword: <REDACTED>'

```

> Ensure to replace 'sakila' with a secure password.

steps.title: Define and deploy a MySQL InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
This step defines the InnoDB Cluster using a Custom Resource and applies it to the cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.28'
EOF

```

> Adjust the version as needed.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.28'
EOF

steps.title: Verify the InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
This step checks if the InnoDB Cluster is created and running successfully.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com

```

> You should see your cluster 'mycluster' listed.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the MySQL Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MySQL Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if not backed up.

```bash

helm uninstall mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl delete namespace mysql-operator

```

> This command removes the MySQL Operator deployment.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the MySQL Operator
upgrade.description:
This step includes backing up your data, upgrading the operator, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com -o yaml > backup-innodbcluster.yaml

helm upgrade mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --version 9.0.1

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> Backup the current InnoDB Cluster configuration.
> Replace '9.0.1' with the desired version.
> Verify that the deployment is updated and running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Error creating InnoDB Cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to incorrect secret configurations or insufficient permissions.

**Fix:**
Check the secret configuration and ensure the MySQL Operator has the necessary permissions.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'MySQL Operator not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the MySQL Operator has not been installed or is not running.

**Fix:**
Reinstall the MySQL Operator using Helm and ensure the deployment is running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to MySQL'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the MySQL root user credentials are incorrect.

**Fix:**
Verify the credentials stored in the Kubernetes secret and update them if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'InnoDB Cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the cluster is still initializing or if there are resource constraints.

**Fix:**
Check the status of the pods in the InnoDB Cluster and ensure sufficient resources are allocated.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for MySQL 8.0, improved backup and restore capabilities.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from version 8.4, ensure to review the new configuration options available.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com' showing your cluster 'mycluster' with a status of 'Ready'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.28'
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary resources.
